title: Dog Green Discharge from Prepuce: Causes and When to See a Vet
meta_desc: Discover what causes green discharge from a dog's prepuce. Learn about smegma, urgency levels, and why triage photos are helpful for diagnosis.
content_html:
What is Preputial Discharge?

Green or yellowish discharge from the prepuce—the sheath covering a dog's penis—is most commonly a substance known as smegma. Smegma is a normal lubricant composed of skin cells, oils, and moisture that helps protect the sensitive tissues. While the color may look concerning, a small amount of discharge is often a normal part of male dog anatomy.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for green discharge is typically Low. If your dog is behaving normally, eating well, and not showing signs of pain, this is rarely an emergency. You should consider a non-emergency veterinary visit if the discharge becomes excessive, has a foul odor, or if your dog is licking the area constantly. Immediate attention is only needed if you notice blood, significant swelling, or if your dog is straining to urinate.

Why a Photo Helps with Triage

Sharing a clear photograph with a triage professional can be extremely beneficial. A photo allows us to see the consistency and volume of the discharge and check the surrounding skin for redness or pustules. This visual information helps distinguish between normal smegma and clinical balanoposthitis (inflammation), ensuring your pet gets the appropriate level of care without unnecessary stress.

faqs.question: Is green discharge always a sign of infection?
faqs.answer: Not necessarily. In many male dogs, a small amount of yellow-green discharge is normal smegma. It only suggests infection if it is accompanied by swelling, redness, or behavioral changes.

faqs.question: Can I clean the area at home?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently wipe the area with a soft, damp cloth or a pet-safe wipe. Avoid using harsh soaps, alcohol, or hydrogen peroxide, as these can irritate the sensitive mucous membranes.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a more serious problem?
faqs.answer: Watch for lethargy, loss of appetite, blood in the urine, or an inability to retract the penis into the sheath. These symptoms require a prompt veterinary examination.
